questions.question: Which is the safest default match type for XLOOKUP?
questions.explanation: XLOOKUP defaults to exact match — that's one of its key advantages over VLOOKUP, where the default is approximate.

questions.question: Which formula returns the total revenue for the West region only?
questions.explanation: SUMIFS syntax: sum_range first, then pairs of (criteria_range, criteria). SUMIF (no S) takes them in a different order — easy to confuse.

questions.question: Which advantage does XLOOKUP have over VLOOKUP?
options: It can look to the left of the lookup column
It has a built-in if_not_found argument
It survives column inserts in the lookup table
questions.explanation: All three are real advantages of XLOOKUP. That's why it has become the modern default for new workbooks.

questions.question: What does =EOMONTH(TODAY(), 0) return?
options: The first day of the current month
The last day of the current month
The last day of the previous month
questions.explanation: EOMONTH(date, 0) returns the last day of the month containing date. Use -1 for previous month, +1 for next month.

questions.question: What does =UNIQUE(Sales[Region]) return in modern Excel?
options: An error — UNIQUE is not a real function
A spilled list of each distinct region, once
questions.explanation: UNIQUE is a dynamic array function (Excel 2021+ / 365). It returns each distinct value once, spilling the result into adjacent cells.

questions.question: Which IFS formula correctly tags revenue as Big / Mid / Small?
questions.explanation: IFS expects (test, value, test, value, ...). End with TRUE as the catch-all condition for the default value.

questions.question: Why is VLOOKUP without FALSE as the last argument considered dangerous?
options: It does an approximate match silently and may return wrong values
It only works on sorted ascending columns even when correct
It is too slow on large datasets
questions.explanation: Without FALSE (or 0), VLOOKUP defaults to approximate match, which assumes the lookup column is sorted ascending. If it isn't, you get wrong answers — silently.
